critic_raw:
This design contains two fundamental flaws.

First, the splitting strategy is patient-unsafe. The design states data is split 'via stratified random sampling on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID.' A diabetic retinopathy study over five years will contain many patients with multiple visits and follow-up images. Splitting by week means a patient photographed in week 3 (training) and week 47 (test) will have their images in different splits. The model learns that patient's retinal morphology during training and exploits that prior at test time. In clinical imaging tasks, patient-level leakage is well-known to inflate AUROC by 5-15 percentage points.

Second, preprocessing is fit on the full dataset. Section 3 states: 'All image preprocessing transformations, including normalization and standardization parameters, are fitted using the entire dataset.' For a medical imaging study with known center-level variation, the normalization statistics reflect the composite distribution of all centers. Test images are normalized using statistics that include their own center's characteristics.

Third, the baseline is 'consensus expert grading'—but the CNN is compared using AUROC, while expert grading produces discrete labels. The CNN has continuous probabilities to optimize its AUROC; the expert baseline has one operating point per grade boundary.
all_issues_raised: Splitting by week rather than patient ID causes patient-level data leakage across train/test splits
Preprocessing normalization parameters fit on entire dataset including test images
AUROC comparison structurally unfair: CNN has continuous probabilities, expert baseline has discrete grade labels
Small minority class (severe: ~250 images) may be insufficient for reliable AUROC estimation

round_transcripts.critic:
The splitting strategy is patient-unsafe. Data is split 'via stratified random sampling on a weekly basis, rather than by patient ID.' Over five years, serial imaging is standard in DR monitoring—the same patient appears in multiple weeks. Splitting by week places the same patient in both train and test. Patient-level leakage in clinical imaging inflates AUROC substantially.

Preprocessing is fit on the full dataset per Section 3—test images contribute to normalization statistics.

The AUROC comparison is structurally unfair: CNN has continuous probabilities; expert baseline has discrete grade labels at one operating point per boundary.
round_transcripts.defender:
On patient-level splitting: diabetic retinopathy screening is typically annual or biannual—most patients contribute 1-2 images over 5 years. If most patients contribute only one image, the weekly split is effectively equivalent to a patient-level split. The risk depends on the cohort's repeat-imaging rate, which is not specified.

On preprocessing: for 5,000 images with 3,500 in training, the test set is only 15% of the data. Its marginal influence on global normalization statistics is small—less than 15% of pixels contributing to channel means.

On AUROC fairness: ordinal expert grades can be used to compute a multi-threshold AUROC approximation (treating each grade boundary as a threshold). This is standard in clinical benchmarking (Gulshan et al., Nature 2016).

round_transcripts.critic:
The patient-level splitting defense relies on an unstated assumption that most patients contribute only one image. Over five years of multi-center DR monitoring, serial imaging is standard practice. The design makes no statement about patient-level deduplication or split enforcement and specifies no sensitivity analysis for this risk. The '1-2 images per patient' assumption is speculative.

On preprocessing: the 'negligible' magnitude argument is not a principled defense—fitting on training only has zero implementation cost. The principle violation is real.

The AUROC fairness concern is partially resolved—ordinal grades can be used to compute multi-threshold AUROC as the defender correctly notes.
round_transcripts.defender:
I concede that the patient-level splitting concern is material and unaddressed by the design. Without explicit documentation of the repeat-imaging rate and without patient-level split enforcement, the design cannot rule out patient-level data leakage.

The preprocessing leakage is a principle violation as the critic correctly states.

The AUROC comparison is defensible and the critic agreed.

all_issues_adjudicated: Splitting by week rather than patient ID causes patient-level data leakage — not addressed by design
Preprocessing normalization parameters fit on entire dataset including test images
